stem: A hospital uses an anti-HCV screening test to screen for hepatitis C. The test has a sensitivity of 86% and a specificity of 94%. The prevalence of hepatitis C in the patient population is estimated to be between 4.4% and 10.2%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has hepatitis C? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(hepatitis C | positive test) ranges from 0.397 to 0.619 as prevalence varies from 4.4% to 10.2%. Point estimate at 7.3% prevalence: 0.53.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.06.
The confidence interval [0.397, 0.619] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (86%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
